currentUse: Active Catholic church — St. Thomas More Parish, the first parish of the Personal Ordinariate of the Chair of St. Peter (former Anglicans/Episcopalians received into the Catholic Church), at 1625 N. Main Avenue / 116 Theodore Street in Scranton's Providence neighborhood; the parish also runs Maria Kaupas Academy, a hybrid classical Catholic school.
summary: The former Šv. Juozapo (St. Joseph's Lithuanian) church building in Scranton's Providence neighborhood is standing and in active liturgical use. The Diocese of Scranton's own consolidated-parishes list records "St. Joseph Lithuanian Church (closed 6/5/2011)" with sacramental records transferred to Mary, Mother of God Parish, Scranton. On May 8, 2012, the St. Thomas More Society — a congregation of former Episcopalians led by ex-Anglican clergyman Fr. Eric Bergman, received into the Catholic Church in 2005 — became the first parish of the U.S. Personal Ordinariate of the Chair of St. Peter and on the same day purchased the St. Joseph's Church property. The parish remains active as of March 2026 with Fr. Bergman still pastor, living in the Providence neighborhood, and operating Maria Kaupas Academy, a hybrid classical Catholic school. Directory listings show a full schedule anchored by a 10:00 a.m. Sunday Solemn Mass. Lithuanian fabric reportedly survives in the building: the memorial stained glass windows, a wayside cross (chapel-post) in the churchyard dedicated to Lithuanians persecuted by the Soviets, and the cornerstone inscribed "Lithuanica Ecclesia." Because the building now belongs to an Ordinariate parish rather than the Diocese of Scranton, it is outside the diocese's ongoing restructuring exposure.

developments.headline: St. Joseph Lithuanian Church formally closed in Diocese of Scranton consolidation
developments.detail: The Diocese of Scranton's consolidated-parishes list (updated 7/3/2024) records 'St. Joseph Lithuanian Church (closed 6/5/2011)' among Scranton churches, with the successor/records parish being Mary, Mother of God Parish, Scranton. The closure came out of the diocese-wide consolidation program announced under Bishop Joseph F. Martino in February 2009.

sources.title: The Diocese Gave Parishes Its Final Word on Consolidations

developments.headline: Property purchased by St. Thomas More, first parish of the U.S. Ordinariate
developments.detail: On May 8, 2012 the St. Thomas More Society — founded 2005 by Anglican clergyman Fr. Eric Bergman and parishioners of the Episcopal Church of the Good Shepherd, received into the Catholic Church on October 31, 2005 — became St. Thomas More Catholic Church, the first parish of the Personal Ordinariate of the Chair of St. Peter, and on the same day purchased the St. Joseph's Church property in the Providence neighborhood of Scranton. No purchase price was published in the sources found.

sources.title: Scranton, Pennsylvania: Saint Thomas More Catholic Church

developments.headline: Parish active and profiled nationally; runs Maria Kaupas Academy
developments.detail: A March 18, 2026 Philanthropy Daily profile describes St. Thomas More as an active parish under founding pastor Fr. Eric Bergman, who lives in the Providence neighborhood, and highlights Maria Kaupas Academy, the parish's hybrid classical Catholic school serving homeschooling families and neighborhood children. Directory listings give a Sunday 10:00 a.m. Solemn Mass and 7:00 p.m. evening Mass plus daily Masses.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Two refinements. (1) Closure date: the archive record says closed 2009 ("Allentown/Scranton sweep"); the Diocese of Scranton's own consolidated-parishes list records the closure as 6/5/2011. The consolidation was announced in February 2009 under Bishop Martino, but the church's formal closing was June 5, 2011 — the archive's 2009 likely reflects the announcement, not the final closure. (2) "Building given to convert-priests": refined — the building was not given but purchased on May 8, 2012 by St. Thomas More Catholic Church, a former-Episcopalian congregation that became the first parish of the Personal Ordinariate of the Chair of St. Peter; its founding pastor Fr. Eric Bergman is an ex-Anglican priest, so "convert-priests" is directionally accurate. The archive's note that the original church was lost to the bishop through the courts and precipitated the LNCC schism is consistent with what was found: True Lithuania describes the 1895-era ownership feud, the litigation loss, and the founding of the Providence of God Lithuanian National Catholic parish (still operating at 1743 N. Sumner Ave, Scranton) as a result.
gaps: Sale price of the 2012 property transfer from the Diocese of Scranton to St. Thomas More was not published in any source found; Lackawanna County deed records were not consulted. The parish's own website (stmscranton.org, including its clergy page) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetching, so the current Mass schedule and staff roster are taken from third-party directories (CatholicMassTime, DiscoverMass) and may be stale — the two directories differ slightly on the weekend schedule. The reported survival of Lithuanian fabric (memorial stained glass, "Lithuanica Ecclesia" cornerstone, wayside cross to Soviet-persecuted Lithuanians) rests on the undated Global True Lithuania page; its current condition was not independently confirmed (the HMDB marker page for the roadside cross also returned 403). Whether Maria Kaupas Academy's name deliberately honors the Lithuanian-American foundress Venerable Maria Kaupas — a fitting continuity if so — was not stated in any source opened. No press coverage of Lithuanian community reunions or preservation campaigns specific to this building was found.
